original_abstract:
Nitride phosphors are essential components for the advancement of white light-emitting diode (w-LED) technologies. In this study, we demonstrate that the luminescent properties of these phosphors can be significantly enhanced through strategic cation substitution, which induces structural modifications, charge variations, and a rearrangement of neighboring nitride clusters. By substituting Ca²⁺ with Sr²⁺, we observed an expansion of the lattice volume and the $bc$ plane, contrasted by a contraction along the $a$-axis. This lattice distortion creates high-coordination environments and sufficient spatial volume around the Sr/Eu centers, facilitating the effective reduction and charge stabilization of activators, as confirmed by X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) spectroscopy.

These structural refinements resulted in a greater than 10% increase in photoluminescent intensity alongside a notable blue shift. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was employed to characterize the microstructural evolution, while solid-state Raman spectroscopy revealed unique shifts in the anion environment following cation substitution. Furthermore, solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy identified specific ordering variations within the SiN₄ and AlN₄ tetrahedra surrounding the divalent centers. This localized structural ordering contributes to a more rigid framework, significantly improving thermal robustness. Specifically, the $Sr_xCa_{0.993-x}AlSiN_3:Eu^{2+}_{0.007}$ phosphor (at $x = 0.9$) exhibited a 10% improvement in thermal stability at 473 K compared to its Sr-free counterpart. These findings highlight the critical role of neighbor-sequence engineering in tetrahedral sites—coordinated by elements such as Li, Mg, Al, and Si—for the design of high-performance phosphors.
humanized_abstract:
Abstract: Cation substitution in nitride phosphors is currently being widely used to advance white light-emitting diode (w-LED) technology. In the present work, we identify the luminescence improvement using cation substitution method, along with the structure changes that accompany the luminescence enhancement. It was found that by substituting Ca²⁺ with Sr²⁺ in the material, leads to an expansion of the unit cell and the bc plane of the wurtzite structure, whereas there is a contraction along the a-axis. As a result, high-coordination sites having sufficient intersite distance around the Sr/Eu species are developed, thus aiding in minimizing the activator species, which is further confirmed by X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) spectroscopy.

Upon these structural modifications, the resulting $Sr_xCa_{0.993-x}AlSiN_3:Eu^{2+}_{0.007}$ sample exhibits a photoluminescent intensity gain exceeding 10% with a clear blue shift compared to that of the pristine phosphor. The evolution of the microstructure is elucidated using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and the solid-state Raman spectra demonstrate the shifts in anion species surrounding the cations upon replacement. The structural information provided by the solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy reveals the variations in the ordering of SiN₄ and AlN₄ tetrahedra surrounding the divalent ions. The structural ordering around the divalent ions is demonstrated to lead to an enhanced structural rigidity, thereby resulting in an improved thermal stability. It is shown that the $Sr_xCa_{0.993-x}AlSiN_3:Eu^{2+}_{0.007}$ phosphor at $x = 0.9$ has 10% enhanced thermal stability at 473 K when compared with that of the $CaAlSiN_3:Eu^{2+}$ sample. The neighbor-sequence engineering of the tetrahedral sites coordinated by the Li, Mg, Al and Si ions is crucial for developing efficient phosphors.

document.input:
Nitride phosphors are essential components for the advancement of white light-emitting diode (w-LED) technologies. In this study, we demonstrate that the luminescent properties of these phosphors can be significantly enhanced through strategic cation substitution, which induces structural modifications, charge variations, and a rearrangement of neighboring nitride clusters. Specifically, the $Sr_xCa_{0.993-x}AlSiN_3:Eu^{2+}_{0.007}$ phosphor (at $x = 0.9$) exhibited a 10% improvement in thermal stability at 473 K compared to its Sr-free counterpart.
